scenario: shows all jurors when no filters are active
whyItMatters: Default state must display all jurors so admins see the full picture before applying any filter.
risk: Hidden jurors by default would make monitoring impossible.

scenario: keeps juror that passes a single group score filter
whyItMatters: Filtering by one group's score range must show only jurors whose score for that group is within range.
risk: Wrong filter logic could hide jurors who should be visible, or show ones who should be hidden.

scenario: hides juror failing one of two active filters (AND logic)
whyItMatters: When multiple group filters are active, a juror must pass ALL of them to remain visible — not just any one.
risk: OR logic instead of AND would show jurors who do not meet all criteria, defeating combined filtering.

scenario: does not apply a filter when min > max (invalid range)
whyItMatters: A filter where minimum exceeds maximum is logically impossible and should be silently ignored.
risk: Applying an impossible range would hide all jurors, completely breaking the view.

scenario: includes score=0 entry (boundary min value)
whyItMatters: A score of exactly zero must be treated as a valid intentional score and must pass a filter that includes 0.
risk: Excluding zero would hide jurors who intentionally gave zero points, silently corrupting visible data.

scenario: includes score=TOTAL_MAX entry (boundary max value)
whyItMatters: A score at the maximum possible value must also pass a filter that includes the maximum boundary.
risk: Off-by-one errors at the upper boundary could exclude perfect scores from filtered results.

scenario: excludes juror whose target group entry is unscored (null total)
whyItMatters: When a score filter is active, jurors who have not scored that group have nothing to compare and must be excluded.
risk: Including unscored entries could make an active filter appear to show incomplete data as if it matched.

scenario: first click on a column → sortMode=group, dir=desc
whyItMatters: First click on a column header should sort descending (highest first) — the most common admin need.
risk: Wrong initial sort direction would confuse admins looking for top or bottom scorers.

scenario: second click on the same column → dir flips to asc
whyItMatters: Second click should flip to ascending so admins can see the lowest scorers.
risk: Broken toggle would force page refresh to change sort direction.

scenario: third click on the same column → sort resets (sortMode=none)
whyItMatters: Third click should return to the original unsorted order so admins can see the default juror list.
risk: No reset path would permanently trap admins in a sorted state with no way back.

scenario: clicking a different column resets cycle to desc on new column
whyItMatters: Switching to a different column must start a fresh sort cycle on the new column.
risk: Inheriting the previous column's sort direction when switching would produce unexpected ordering.

scenario: desc sort orders higher scores first
whyItMatters: Descending sort must put the highest-scoring juror at the top of the list.
risk: Reversed sort order would completely mislead admins comparing juror performance.

scenario: asc sort orders lower scores first
whyItMatters: Ascending sort must put the lowest-scoring juror at the top.
risk: Reversed sort order would mislead admins looking for underperforming evaluations.

scenario: matches on juror name (case insensitive)
whyItMatters: Admins can quickly find a specific juror by typing part of their name, regardless of capitalisation.
risk: Case-sensitive search would require exact typing, making lookup error-prone.

scenario: matches on juror dept
whyItMatters: Admins can filter the grid to show only jurors from a specific department.
risk: Department search not working would force scrolling through all jurors manually.

scenario: OR logic — matches jurors whose name OR dept contains the query
whyItMatters: A search query should match jurors regardless of whether it hits the name or the department field.
risk: AND logic would only match jurors whose name AND department both contain the query, reducing results too aggressively.

scenario: no match → empty visible list
whyItMatters: A search with no matching jurors should show an empty list, not a crash or stale results.
risk: Showing stale results when no match is found would give admins false information.

scenario: builds lookup map and averages from completed jurors only
whyItMatters: The score grid average row must reflect only finalized jurors — not partially completed or editing ones.
risk: Including incomplete scores in averages would produce misleading published results.

scenario: excludes juror with editEnabled=true from average
whyItMatters: A juror currently re-editing must not count toward the published average while their data is in flux.
risk: Including editing jurors could push averages up or down while an evaluation is being revised.

scenario: includes juror with finalSubmitted=true and no finalSubmittedAt [Fix 2 regression]
whyItMatters: A juror marked final-submitted must be counted in averages even if the submission timestamp is missing.
risk: This regression previously caused valid submitted jurors to be silently dropped from averages.

scenario: returns null averages when all jurors are in editing mode
whyItMatters: When every juror is in edit mode, there is no finalized data and averages should show blank.
risk: Displaying stale or partial averages when everyone is editing would mislead the admin dashboard.

scenario: excludes partial-state entries from average (no total)
whyItMatters: An incomplete evaluation (some criteria filled, no final total) must not pollute published averages.
risk: Including partial scores would undercount the average and distort group rankings.

scenario: includes zero-score entry in average (total=0 is scored) [zero regression]
whyItMatters: A group intentionally scored 0 must be included in the average. Zero is a valid score.
risk: This regression caused zero scores to be treated as missing, inflating averages by silently dropping them.

scenario: computes correct numeric average across multiple jurors
whyItMatters: The arithmetic mean across multiple jurors must be calculated and rounded to two decimal places.
risk: Rounding errors in the average calculation would produce incorrect published group scores.

scenario: applies dense ranking with ties and excludes non-finalized groups
whyItMatters: Tied projects must share the same rank number. Unfinished projects must not appear in rankings.
risk: Incorrect ranking or showing incomplete projects would misrepresent results to faculty.

scenario: rankMap is stable when search filters the visible list [Fix 1 regression]
whyItMatters: Filtering the visible list must not recalculate ranks. A group ranked 4th must stay 4th even when viewed alone.
risk: This regression previously caused a searched group to appear as rank 1, which is factually wrong.

scenario: exports currently filtered/sorted list
whyItMatters: The Excel export button must export exactly the filtered and sorted list currently visible on screen.
risk: Exporting the unfiltered list would give admins and faculty incorrect export data.

scenario: filters rows by score status
whyItMatters: Admins need to isolate completed (scored) evaluations from in-progress ones in the detail table.
risk: Broken filter would force admins to scan all rows manually to find scored evaluations.

scenario: filters rows by juror status
whyItMatters: Admins need to see only rows matching a specific juror workflow state (e.g. Editing).
risk: Broken filter would make it impossible to monitor which jurors have active edit sessions.

scenario: filters rows by updated date range (from)
whyItMatters: Admins can narrow the detail table to evaluations updated after a certain date.
risk: Broken date filter would force manual scanning of all rows to find recent activity.

scenario: does not apply updated date filter when range is invalid
whyItMatters: When 'from' date is later than 'to', the filter must be rejected with an error and all rows kept visible.
risk: Applying an impossible date range would silently hide all rows with no explanation.

scenario: sorts newer year before older year
whyItMatters: Most recent semesters must appear at the top so admins do not have to scroll to find the current period.
risk: Reversed year order would bury the active semester at the bottom of a long list.

scenario: sorts Fall before Spring within the same year
whyItMatters: Within the same year, Fall semester is chronologically after Spring and should appear first.
risk: Wrong term ordering would cause admins to select the wrong semester when periods have similar names.

scenario: sorts Fall > Summer > Spring > Winter within the same year
whyItMatters: All four academic terms must be ordered correctly by academic calendar within the same year.
risk: Incorrect term ordering forces admins to hunt for the right semester, increasing risk of wrong selection.

scenario: puts semester with no year at the end
whyItMatters: Pilot or custom semesters without a year must not displace real semesters.
risk: Unnamed semesters appearing first would push active real semesters out of view.

scenario: calls onSetActive with the selected semester id when dropdown changes
whyItMatters: Changing the active semester dropdown must correctly trigger the backend update with the right semester ID.
risk: Sending the wrong ID would activate the wrong semester, misrouting all ongoing evaluations.

scenario: disables delete button for the active semester
whyItMatters: The currently active semester cannot be deleted while evaluations are in progress.
risk: Deleting the active semester would destroy all ongoing evaluation data for the current period.

scenario: enables delete button for a non-active semester
whyItMatters: Inactive semesters can be deleted for cleanup — the delete button must be enabled for them.
risk: Blocking all deletes would prevent admins from cleaning up old semesters.

scenario: creates semester with valid input
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to create new semesters for each academic period.
risk: Broken creation would prevent starting new evaluation cycles.

scenario: calls delete callback for non-active semester
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove outdated semesters to keep the list manageable.
risk: Broken delete would leave orphaned semesters accumulating over time.

scenario: does not submit duplicate semester name
whyItMatters: Two semesters with the same name would cause confusion in the evaluation workflow.
risk: Duplicate semesters could cause jurors to submit scores to the wrong period.

scenario: shows create field error from API response
whyItMatters: If the server rejects a semester creation, the error must be displayed to the admin.
risk: Silent API errors would make admins think the semester was created when it was not.

scenario: shows error when file exceeds 2MB [Fix 6 regression]
whyItMatters: Files larger than 2MB are likely wrong files and must be rejected immediately.
risk: Accepting oversized files could hang the browser or upload corrupted data to the system.

scenario: shows error when required header columns are missing
whyItMatters: A CSV file without the required column headers cannot be correctly parsed and must be rejected.
risk: Importing a CSV with wrong headers would silently create jurors with no names or institutions.

scenario: shows error when juror_name is blank
whyItMatters: A juror without a name cannot be identified, contacted, or tracked in the system.
risk: Unnamed jurors would break the evaluation assignment workflow and produce unreadable reports.

scenario: shows error when juror_inst is blank
whyItMatters: Institution is required for juror identification and correct PIN tracking.
risk: Jurors without institutions cannot be uniquely identified when two jurors share a name.

scenario: shows error when same juror appears twice in the CSV
whyItMatters: Duplicate rows in a single file must be caught before import to prevent double-counting jury members.
risk: Silent duplicates would create two records for the same person, causing scoring anomalies.

scenario: treats existing system jurors as a warning, not an error
whyItMatters: Re-uploading a CSV that includes already-imported jurors should warn, not block — enabling safe incremental imports.
risk: Treating existing jurors as errors would prevent admins from using a master juror CSV file.

scenario: calls onResetPin with correct juror data when PIN button is clicked
whyItMatters: PIN reset must pass the correct juror object to the backend so the right account is updated.
risk: Sending the wrong juror ID would reset the wrong person's PIN, locking them out.

scenario: calls onDeleteJuror when delete button is clicked
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove incorrectly added jurors from the system.
risk: Broken delete would force database-level cleanup outside the admin panel.

scenario: calls onAddJuror with name and inst when create form is submitted
whyItMatters: The add juror form must submit both name and institution to create a complete juror record.
risk: Omitting either field at submission time would create incomplete records despite the UI showing them.

scenario: shows error when file exceeds 2MB [Fix 6 regression]
whyItMatters: Files larger than 2MB are almost certainly wrong files and must be blocked before parsing.
risk: Processing an oversized file could hang or corrupt the group list.

scenario: shows error when required header columns are missing
whyItMatters: A groups CSV without required headers cannot produce valid group records.
risk: Importing a malformed CSV would create groups with no number, title, or students.

scenario: shows error when group_no is zero
whyItMatters: Group numbers must be positive integers. Zero is not a valid group number.
risk: A group numbered 0 would be unidentifiable in the evaluation system.

scenario: shows error when group_no is negative
whyItMatters: Negative group numbers are not valid and would break sorting and display logic.
risk: Negative numbers would appear incorrectly in the UI and exports.

scenario: shows error when group_no is non-numeric
whyItMatters: Group numbers must be numeric — text values would break any comparison or sorting logic.
risk: Non-numeric group numbers could crash downstream logic that expects integers.

scenario: warns about invalid separator when students are comma-separated in CSV field
whyItMatters: Students must be separated by semicolons. Comma separation inside a CSV field corrupts the student list.
risk: Comma-separated students would be split incorrectly, creating partial names as student records.

scenario: shows error for duplicate group_no within the same CSV
whyItMatters: Two rows with the same group number in one file would create conflicting group records.
risk: Duplicate group numbers would cause evaluation scores to be assigned to the wrong project.

scenario: accepts valid semicolon-separated students (quoted CSV field)
whyItMatters: A correctly formatted CSV with semicolon-separated students inside a quoted field must import successfully.
risk: Rejecting valid files would force admins to manually enter each group one by one.

scenario: calls onDeleteProject when delete is clicked
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove incorrectly imported groups from the system.
risk: Broken delete would leave wrong groups in the evaluation system, causing jury confusion.

scenario: shows Unlock Editing button for a completed, non-editing juror
whyItMatters: Only jurors who have fully submitted and are not currently editing can be re-opened for corrections.
risk: Showing the unlock button for ineligible jurors could open evaluations that are still in progress.

scenario: does not show Unlock Editing when evalLockActive is true
whyItMatters: The system-wide evaluation lock must take absolute priority — no individual unlock is possible while it is active.
risk: Allowing unlocks while the global lock is on would break the centralised control mechanism.

scenario: does not show Unlock Editing when juror has not submitted (not completed)
whyItMatters: Jurors who have not yet submitted cannot be re-opened for editing.
risk: Unlocking an unsubmitted juror would create an undefined workflow state.

scenario: checkbox is checked when evalLockActive=true
whyItMatters: The global evaluation lock checkbox must accurately reflect current system state.
risk: An unchecked checkbox when the lock is active would give admins false confidence that edits are allowed.

scenario: calls onRequestEvalLockChange(true) when unchecked checkbox is clicked
whyItMatters: Clicking an unchecked lock checkbox must request activation of the global evaluation lock.
risk: Calling with wrong argument (false instead of true) would toggle the lock in the wrong direction.

scenario: shows Lock Editing button when juror is in edit mode
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to see which jurors have open editing sessions and act on them.
risk: Missing button would leave editing sessions open indefinitely with no admin recourse.

scenario: calls onForceCloseEdit with jurorId when Lock Editing is clicked
whyItMatters: Force-closing an editing session must pass the correct juror ID to the backend.
risk: Wrong juror ID would close the wrong session, leaving the intended session open.

scenario: shows juror matching name substring
whyItMatters: Quick name lookup is essential when managing permissions for a large number of jurors.
risk: Broken search would force admins to scroll through the entire list to find one juror.

scenario: "lock editing" query matches only jurors in edit mode
whyItMatters: Typing 'lock editing' acts as a smart filter to show all jurors currently in an editing session.
risk: Broken semantic search would make it impossible to get a quick list of open editing sessions.

scenario: "unlock editing" query matches only completed jurors not in edit mode
whyItMatters: Typing 'unlock editing' shows only jurors eligible for re-opening — a quick action list.
risk: Wrong match logic could show ineligible jurors, leading to accidental permission changes.

scenario: shows error when new password is shorter than 10 characters
whyItMatters: Admin passwords must be at least 10 characters to meet minimum security policy.
risk: Short passwords are easily brute-forced, exposing all jury evaluation data.

scenario: shows error when new password has no uppercase letter
whyItMatters: Uppercase letters are required as part of the password complexity policy.
risk: Lowercase-only passwords reduce the character space and are easier to guess.

scenario: shows error when new password has no digit
whyItMatters: A digit is required in admin passwords per complexity policy.
risk: Passwords without digits are weaker against dictionary attacks.

scenario: shows error when new password has no symbol
whyItMatters: A special character is required in admin passwords per complexity policy.
risk: Passwords without symbols have significantly fewer possible combinations.

scenario: shows error when new and confirm passwords do not match
whyItMatters: The confirmation field prevents typos from locking the admin out of the system.
risk: A typo in the new password field with no confirmation check would set an unknown password.

scenario: calls onPasswordChanged after valid form submission
whyItMatters: A valid password change must call the completion callback with the new password so the session is updated.
risk: Not calling the callback would leave the session using the old password despite the change appearing successful.

scenario: renders placeholders and hides rings when there is no data
whyItMatters: The dashboard must degrade gracefully when no evaluations have started, showing a clear empty state.
risk: Crashes or broken UI on empty data would make the panel unusable at the start of a new semester.

scenario: clamps ring percentage to 100%
whyItMatters: Progress rings must never display more than 100% even if the data has an inconsistency.
risk: An overflowing ring graphic would break the visual layout and confuse stakeholders.

scenario: renders nothing when metrics is null
whyItMatters: The completion bar must not crash when no metrics data is provided.
risk: A crash here would break the entire Overview tab before any evaluations begin.

scenario: renders completion text with correct counts
whyItMatters: The completion bar must show the accurate number of completed vs total jurors.
risk: Wrong counts would give faculty an inaccurate picture of evaluation progress.

scenario: shows pending count when some jurors are incomplete
whyItMatters: The pending count helps admins quickly see how many jurors still need to finish.
risk: Missing pending count would require manual subtraction to track outstanding evaluations.

scenario: renders 100% completion without pending
whyItMatters: At full completion, the 'pending' label must not appear — all evaluations are done.
risk: Showing a phantom pending message at 100% would alarm faculty unnecessarily.

scenario: clamps completion % to 100 when completedJurors > totalJurors
whyItMatters: The progress bar fill must never exceed 100% width even with inconsistent data.
risk: An overflowing bar would break the layout and look broken to faculty.

scenario: renders without throwing when both props are empty arrays
whyItMatters: The juror activity list must render safely when no jurors or groups exist yet.
risk: A crash on empty data would break the Overview tab at the start of every new semester.

scenario: renders without throwing when props are omitted (uses defaults)
whyItMatters: The component must have safe defaults so it does not crash when a parent forgets to pass props.
risk: Missing prop defaults cause crashes that are hard to reproduce in development.

scenario: shows search input for filtering jurors
whyItMatters: The juror activity panel must include a search field so admins can quickly find a specific juror.
risk: Missing search input would force manual scanning of the full juror list.

scenario: renders without throwing with empty/null data
whyItMatters: The analytics tab must not crash when loaded with no data — it is shown immediately on navigation.
risk: A crash on empty data would make the Analytics tab permanently unavailable at semester start.

scenario: renders a loading indicator when loading=true
whyItMatters: When data is being fetched, the tab must display a loading state without crashing.
risk: A crash during loading would prevent admins from ever seeing analytics while data loads.

scenario: returns 'empty' for null or undefined entry
whyItMatters: A null or missing score entry must be identified as empty so the UI shows the correct placeholder.
risk: Misidentifying empty entries could cause wrong status labels and confuse jury workflow tracking.

scenario: returns 'empty' when no total and no criteria are set
whyItMatters: An entry where all fields are null must be classified as empty, not partial.
risk: Treating all-null as partial would miscount scored/unscored evaluations in progress reporting.

scenario: returns 'scored' when total is a positive number
whyItMatters: A positive total indicates a completed evaluation and must be shown as scored.
risk: Missing the scored state would prevent workflow completion detection from working.

story: Cell State Detection — Zero Score Regression
scenario: returns 'scored' when total is zero (zero is a valid intentional score)
whyItMatters: A score of zero is an intentional evaluation result and must be recognised as scored, not empty.
risk: This was a real regression that caused zero-score evaluations to appear incomplete, blocking workflow completion.

scenario: returns 'partial' when at least one criterion is filled but no total
whyItMatters: Partially filled evaluations must be shown as in-progress so jurors know they still need to complete.
risk: Showing partial entries as empty would hide work in progress from both jurors and admins.

scenario: returns 'partial' when multiple criteria are filled but no total
whyItMatters: Multiple criteria filled without a total still means the evaluation is not complete.
risk: Misidentifying multi-criterion partial entries could cause premature workflow advancement.

scenario: returns 0 for null or undefined entry
whyItMatters: A missing score entry must produce a running total of 0 without throwing an error.
risk: A crash on null input would break the live score display for any juror who has not started.

scenario: sums only numeric criteria values
whyItMatters: Only filled numeric criteria must count toward the running total — null fields must be skipped.
risk: Summing nulls or treating them as 0 would undercount the running total while scoring.

scenario: includes zero-value criteria in the sum
whyItMatters: A criteria value of 0 is explicitly set and must count toward the running total.
risk: Skipping zero values would produce an incorrect sum and mislead the juror about their progress.

scenario: returns full score when all criteria are filled
whyItMatters: When all criteria are entered, the partial total should equal the complete score total.
risk: A discrepancy between partial total and final total would confuse jurors about their score.

scenario: ignores string and null values
whyItMatters: Invalid or corrupt field values must be ignored rather than causing a crash.
risk: Summing strings would produce NaN, breaking the entire live score display.

scenario: returns 'editing' when editEnabled is true, regardless of other state
whyItMatters: Edit mode overrides all other states — a juror re-editing must always show as 'editing'.
risk: Showing 'completed' while a juror is actively editing would mislead the admin dashboard.

scenario: returns 'completed' when juror is in finalMap and not editing
whyItMatters: Final submission must be correctly detected to count the juror toward completion metrics.
risk: Miscounting completed jurors would give faculty wrong progress numbers.

scenario: returns 'ready_to_submit' when all groups are scored and not finalized
whyItMatters: When all groups are scored but the juror has not submitted, they must see the submit prompt.
risk: Missing 'ready_to_submit' state would leave jurors stranded without a clear call to action.

scenario: returns 'in_progress' when some groups are started but not all scored
whyItMatters: Partially completed evaluations must be tracked as in-progress to guide the juror.
risk: Showing in-progress as ready-to-submit would offer a premature submit button.

scenario: returns 'not_started' when no scores exist
whyItMatters: A juror who has not scored any group must be shown as not yet started.
risk: Incorrect initial state would give admins a wrong picture of who has begun scoring.

scenario: returns 'not_started' when group list is empty
whyItMatters: If no groups are defined yet, the juror cannot have started — must default to not_started.
risk: A crash or wrong state on empty group list would break the panel at semester setup time.

scenario: all jury step components render without throwing
whyItMatters: A crash on any step makes the entire evaluation day impossible for jurors.
risk: Undetected import or prop errors would surface only at evaluation time with no fallback.

scenario: shows remaining attempts on first failed PIN entry
whyItMatters: Jurors need immediate feedback on failed PINs to know how many tries remain before lockout.
risk: Missing attempt counter leaves jurors unaware they are about to be locked out.

scenario: lockout screen shown when error_code is locked
whyItMatters: After 3 failed attempts the UI must switch to the locked state to prevent further attempts.
risk: Failing to show the lockout screen would allow continued attempts and confuse the juror.

scenario: attempt counter decrements on each failed attempt
whyItMatters: Each failed attempt must reduce the displayed counter so jurors can track remaining tries accurately.
risk: A static counter would mislead jurors about how close they are to lockout.

scenario: score below 0 is clamped to 0 on blur
whyItMatters: Negative scores are invalid and must be corrected silently before writing to the database.
risk: Allowing negative values would corrupt the total score and ranking calculations.

scenario: score above max is clamped to max on blur
whyItMatters: Scores exceeding the criterion maximum inflate totals and must be rejected.
risk: Unclamped high scores would break rankings and violate rubric constraints.

scenario: fully-scored juror resuming session does not get auto-submit confirmation
whyItMatters: A juror who already scored all groups and returns to check progress must not be immediately forced into a submission dialog.
risk: Without the guard, every resume of a complete session would trigger an unexpected final-submission prompt.

scenario: SemesterStep auto-advances when only one semester is available
whyItMatters: Forcing jurors to manually select a semester when there is only one wastes time and adds unnecessary friction on evaluation day.
risk: Missing the auto-advance logic would leave jurors stuck on a selection screen with no obvious action.

scenario: juror chip shows Completed when allSubmitted is true
whyItMatters: Jurors who have already submitted must see a clear Completed status so they know their work is done.
risk: Wrong chip label could cause a juror to re-submit or be confused about their submission state.

scenario: juror chip shows In Progress when partial score data exists
whyItMatters: Returning jurors must immediately see their previous progress to decide whether to resume or start fresh.
risk: Displaying the wrong chip would mislead jurors about their current evaluation state.

scenario: EvalStep renders without throwing with minimal props
whyItMatters: A crash on the scoring screen would block all evaluations for all jurors.
risk: Any prop-shape change or missing dependency silently breaks the scoring UI.

scenario: Prev button is disabled when current group is the first
whyItMatters: Navigating before the first group would result in an out-of-bounds project index.
risk: An enabled Prev button on group 0 could cause the UI to render undefined project data.

scenario: Next button is disabled when current group is the last
whyItMatters: Navigating past the last group would result in an out-of-bounds project index.
risk: An enabled Next button on the final group could crash the scoring form.

scenario: Submit All button is shown when allComplete is true and editMode is false
whyItMatters: Jurors must be able to submit only after scoring all groups — early submission risks incomplete data.
risk: Button appearing prematurely could allow partial submissions; missing button blocks completion.

scenario: Score inputs are disabled and lock banner is shown when lockActive is true
whyItMatters: After the semester is locked, jurors must not be able to modify submitted scores.
risk: An enabled input in locked state could silently accept edits that are then rejected by the backend.

scenario: All scores saved banner is shown when groupSynced is true and editMode is false
whyItMatters: Jurors need visual confirmation that their scores were persisted before navigating away.
risk: Missing the banner could leave jurors uncertain whether their data was saved.

scenario: Thank You title includes the juror name after submission
whyItMatters: Personalised confirmation reassures the juror that their submission was accepted.
risk: A missing or broken title could be confused with an error state.

scenario: Edit mode title is shown when onEditScores prop is provided
whyItMatters: Jurors re-entering the scoring screen must immediately know they are in edit mode.
risk: Showing the Thank You title in edit mode could confuse the juror into thinking they already submitted.

scenario: Edit My Scores button is shown when onEditScores prop is provided and hidden otherwise
whyItMatters: Edit access is controlled by the admin — the button must only appear when the admin has enabled it.
risk: A visible edit button when editing is disabled could confuse or mislead jurors.

scenario: Total score is shown correctly for a fully scored project
whyItMatters: Jurors review their submitted totals on the done screen to verify accuracy.
risk: An incorrect total could erode trust in the system or cause disputes.
